narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 42% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is higher than what we typically accept to ensure student success. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your fourth-decile ranking in Year 1 grades—below the typical student's mid-fifth decile—and your sixth-decile standing in Year 3, which is just above average but still signaled some risk. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 also sits a bit under the average of 3.21, contributing to the model's concerns about your overall preparation. While your LSAT score of 37 outperforms the average of 36.7, and your upper-middle family income exceeds typical levels, these didn't outweigh the red flags for the model. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, followed closely by your lower Year 1 decile, then your LSAT, family income, racial background as White (which is common in the dataset), undergraduate GPA, part-time enrollment status (rarer than the usual full-time path), with your gender as male providing the only mild counterbalance toward expecting success. In comparison to typical students—who often have stronger early-year deciles, full-time commitments, and balanced GPAs—your profile showed a weaker start that the model weighed heavily, even as later improvements and test scores offered some positives. This ranking of influences highlights how past academic deciles dominated the decision over things like test scores or background. The good news is your trajectory improved from Year 1 to Year 3, suggesting potential if built upon. To boost your future bar exam odds, consider committing to full-time study to match the majority path, focus on elevating your GPA through targeted coursework or tutoring, and refine study habits early to avoid low deciles. Retaking the LSAT for an even higher score could also help tip predictions in your favor next time. Many students in similar spots succeed by addressing these gaps—keep pursuing your goal with these adjustments.
